questions.question_text: What is the maximum security deposit a Connecticut landlord can collect from a tenant under age 62?
questions.explanation: Connecticut limits security deposits to 2 months' rent for tenants under age 62. Tenants age 62 or older have a lower cap of 1 month's rent.

questions.question_text: A 65-year-old tenant rents an apartment for $1,500/month. The maximum security deposit is:
questions.explanation: For tenants age 62 or older, the security deposit is capped at 1 month's rent ($1,500). The standard 2-month cap applies only to tenants under 62.

questions.question_text: A Connecticut landlord must hold a tenant's security deposit in:
questions.correct_answer: An escrow account at a Connecticut bank
distractors: Any bank account in the landlord's name
questions.explanation: Security deposits must be held in an escrow account at a Connecticut bank. The landlord must also pay annual interest to the tenant at the rate set by the Banking Commissioner.

questions.question_text: After a tenant moves out, the landlord must return the security deposit within:
questions.explanation: PA 23-207 shortened the deadline: the landlord must return the deposit, with a written statement itemizing any deductions, not later than 21 days after the tenancy ends — or 15 days after receiving the tenant's written forwarding address, whichever is later. Violations expose the landlord to double damages. CGS § 47a-21(d).

questions.question_text: A landlord withholds $500 from a $3,000 security deposit for cleaning. The tenant must receive:
distractors: $3,000 since cleaning is not a valid deduction
$2,500 only if they request a statement
questions.explanation: The landlord must deliver the remaining $2,500 with a written statement itemizing deductions within 21 days of termination (or 15 days after receiving the tenant's forwarding address, if later) under CGS § 47a-21(d). Conditions noted on the pre-occupancy walk-through checklist cannot be deducted (CGS § 47a-7c).

questions.question_text: Under PA 23-207, the maximum late fee for overdue rent in Connecticut is:
distractors: $5 per day with no maximum cap applied
Whatever amount the signed lease specifies
questions.explanation: PA 23-207 caps late fees at $5 per day up to $50 maximum, or 5% of the overdue rent — whichever is less. Landlords can only charge one late fee per delinquent rent payment.

questions.question_text: A monthly tenant's rent is due on the 1st. Under Connecticut law, a late fee may be assessed only if rent is still unpaid after:
questions.explanation: CGS § 47a-15a: a tenant has nine days after the due date to pay (through the 10th for rent due the 1st); a late charge may apply only to a payment made after the grace period. Weekly tenancies get four days. PA 23-207 capped the charge itself.

questions.question_text: A Connecticut landlord charges a $75 application processing fee to a prospective tenant. This is:
distractors: Legal if refunded upon lease signing
questions.explanation: CGS § 47a-4d (PA 23-207) bars any fee for processing, reviewing or accepting a rental application. A landlord may collect only a security deposit, first month's rent, a key or special-equipment deposit, and a capped tenant screening report fee — and no move-in or move-out fees.

questions.question_text: Under PA 23-207, the base cap (before annual CPI adjustments) on a Connecticut tenant screening report fee is:
questions.explanation: CGS § 47a-4d(c) caps the screening report fee at $50 plus an annual CPI adjustment determined by the Commissioner of Housing, and the landlord must give the applicant a copy of the report. No other rental application processing fees are permitted.

questions.question_text: A weekly tenant's rent is due on Monday. The grace period before late fees can accrue is:
questions.explanation: Weekly leases have a 4-day grace period under PA 23-207, compared to the 9-day grace period for monthly leases. Late fees cannot be charged during the grace period.

questions.question_text: Under Connecticut's eviction record sealing law, an eviction case that is dismissed must be removed from the court website within:
questions.explanation: Cases that are withdrawn, dismissed, or decided in the tenant's favor must be removed from the court website within 30 days. This protects tenants from being penalized by future landlords for unsuccessful eviction attempts.

questions.question_text: A landlord raises rent significantly 2 months after a tenant filed a health code complaint. This could constitute:
distractors: A fair rent commission matter only
questions.explanation: Connecticut prohibits retaliatory actions (including rent increases) within 6 months of a tenant complaint. A significant rent increase shortly after a complaint raises a strong presumption of retaliation.

questions.question_text: Tenants age 62 or older in buildings with 5 or more units have protection against:
questions.explanation: Connecticut provides special eviction protections for tenants age 62+ and disabled tenants in buildings with 5 or more units. These tenants cannot be evicted without good cause, providing housing stability.

questions.question_text: Before moving in, a Connecticut tenant has the right to:
questions.correct_answer: A pre-move-in walkthrough with the landlord
distractors: A professional home inspection at landlord expense
A 3-day cooling-off period after signing the lease
Renegotiate rent based on property condition
questions.explanation: Tenants have the right to a walkthrough with the landlord before moving in. Damage documented during this walkthrough cannot be deducted from the security deposit upon move-out.

questions.question_text: Connecticut municipalities may maintain Fair Rent Commissions to:
questions.correct_answer: Review complaints about excessive rent increases
distractors: Set maximum rent for all residential units
Approve all rent increases before they take effect
Provide rent subsidies to qualifying tenants
questions.explanation: Connecticut has no statewide rent control, but CGS § 7-148b permits any municipality to create a fair rent commission and, since PA 22-30, requires one in every municipality of 25,000 or more residents. Commissions investigate complaints and may order excessive rents reduced.
